expectations.text: A sprite PNG file is delivered in the outputs directory
The sprite has a transparent background (all four corner pixels are fully transparent)
The sprite is item-sized: longest side is between 8 and 32 pixels
The sprite uses at most 16 unique opaque colors (pixel-art palette discipline)
Every opaque color in the sprite is an exact color from the house palette (assets/palette.txt)
Alpha is hard: every pixel is fully opaque or fully transparent (no semi-transparent fringe)
If web images were used as reference, attribution/license information is preserved in the outputs
expectations.evidence: no downloaded web images in outputs (vacuous)

observations: The new skill passed 20/20 assertions; the old skill passed 13/20. Every old-skill failure is a style-consistency failure: off-palette colors (all 3 evals), too many unique colors (chest: 62, castle: 1652), and soft alpha fringes (chest, candle). These are exactly the properties the deterministic pipeline guarantees by construction.

Discriminating assertions: exact-palette match, max-16-colors, hard alpha. Non-discriminating (passed by both configurations): sprite delivered, transparent corners, size ranges, SVG validity, attribution — these verify baseline competence but don't separate the versions.

Qualitative nuance the pass rates hide: the old skill's hand-drawn chest and candle are charming, arguably prettier as standalone icons than the converted ones. But they are non-reproducible one-offs (the same prompt would yield a different sprite every time), cost more tokens, and silently break palette cohesion - the user's stated goal.

Castle eval: the old skill delivered the input render unchanged (1,652 colors, anti-aliased alpha), judging it already compliant under its own lax 'full RGB, anti-aliasing encouraged' rules. Test-design caveat: the castle input is the same render the skill's examples derive from, which the old-skill agent noticed.

Time/tokens are comparable (182s vs 181s, 27k vs 30k tokens). The new skill's high time variance (±76s) comes from the chest run's web-search loop: the first Openverse query returned unusable photos and the skill's query-refinement fallback ('treasure chest clipart') triggered correctly - evidence the fallback path works, at a wall-clock cost.
Single run per configuration per eval, so no variance data within evals; treat per-eval deltas as directional.
